coredata.dc:title: Development of nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped reduced graphene oxide from waste plastic for supercapacitor applications: Comparative electrochemical performance in different electrolytes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Rawat, Kundan Singh
Tewari, Chetna
Arya, Tanuja
Kim, Young Nam
Pant, Prabhat
Sati, Satish
Dhali, Sunil
Negi, Pushpa Bhakuni
Jung, Yong Chae
Sahoo, Nanda Gopal
coredata.dc:description: The persistent non-biodegradable nature of plastic highlights the urgent need for effective waste management and resource conservation, underscoring the crucial importance of recycling and upcycling within a cradle-to-cradle framework. This research introduces an eco-friendly and straightforward upcycling process for plastic waste, which produces significant quantities of reduced graphene oxide through a carefully designed 2-stage pyrolysis method. To enhance the electrochemical properties of the reduced graphene oxide, they were doped with heteroatoms (i.e. nitrogen and phosphorus) via a hydrothermal route. Also, as the nature of the electrolyte plays a significant role in electrochemical analysis, a comparative evaluation of the supercapacitive performance of the heteroatom-doped reduced graphene oxide was conducted across various aqueous electrolytes, including 1 M H2SO4, 6 M KOH, and 2 M KCl, as well as hydrogel polymer electrolytes such as 1 M H2SO4/1 M PVA, 2 M KCl/1 M PVA, and 6 M KOH/1 M PVA. Our results demonstrate that synthesized material from waste plastic exhibits excellent performance, particularly when combined with a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, achieving the highest specific capacitance of 407.6 F/g. In conclusion, this study presents a cost-effective and sustainable approach to promoting a circular economy by repurposing waste plastic for energy storage applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-doping
Electrolytes
Supercapacitor
Reduced graphene oxide
Waste plastic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cyclic stability and percentage capacitance retention

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Method for synthesis of nitrogen- and phosphorous-doped reduced graphene oxide.

(a) Raman spectra of WPrGO and WPrGO-NP, highlighting the comparative ID/IG ratio indicative of defects. (b) XRD patterns of WPrGO and WPrGO-NP, illustrating the amorphous nature of both materials. WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide; WPrGO-NP = nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO; XRD = X-ray diffraction.

TGA analysis of WPrGO and WPrGO-NP, illustrating their comparative thermal stability. TGA = thermogravimetric analysis; WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide; WPrGO-NP = nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO.

Full-range XPS analysis of (a) WPrGO and (b) WPrGO-NP, with high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s region for (c) WPrGO and (d) WPrGO-NP. WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide; WPrGO-NP = nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO; XPS = X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

High resolution SEM and TEM images of (a, c) WPrGO and (b, d) WPrGO-NP. SEM = scanning electron microscopy; TEM = transmission electron microscopy; WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide; WPrGO-NP = nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of WPrGO-NP in different electrolytes: (a) 1 M H2SO4, (b) 1 M H2SO4/1 M PVA, (c) 2 M KCl, (d) 2 M KCl/1 M PVA, (e) 6 M KOH, and (f) 6 M KOH/1 M PVA. WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide; WPrGO-NP = nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO.

Galvanostatic Charge-Discharge (GCD) profiles of WPrGO-NP in various electrolytes: (a) 1 M H2SO4, (b) 1 M H2SO4/1 M PVA, (c) 2 M KCl, (d) 2 M KCl/1 M PVA, (e) 6 M KOH, and (f) 6 M KOH/1 M PVA. WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis of WPrGO-NP, illustrating the impact of different electrolytes on the charge transfer resistance and overall electrochemical behavior: (a) 1 M H2SO4, and 1 M H2SO4/1 M PVA, (b) 2 M KCl, and 2 M KCl/1 M PVA, and (c) 6 M KOH, and 6 M KOH/1 M PVA. WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide.

(a) Cyclic stability of WPrGO-NP represented by specific capacitance (Cs) values at 10 A/g current density. (b) Cyclic stability illustrated by percentage capacitance retention at 10 A/g current density. WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide.
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) data of WPrGO-NP measured in various electrolytes

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide; WPrGO-NP = nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO.
The bold values represent the highest values.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Galvanostatic charge-discharge data of WPrGO-NP derived from plastic waste, recorded in different electrolytes

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: WPrGO = waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide; WPrGO-NP = nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO.
The bold values represent the highest values.

head.ce:title: Development of nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped reduced graphene oxide from waste plastic for supercapacitor applications: Comparative electrochemical performance in different electrolytes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Graphic Era Hill University, Bhimtal Campus, Nainital, Uttarakhand, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The persistent non-biodegradable nature of plastic highlights the urgent need for effective waste management and resource conservation, underscoring the crucial importance of recycling and upcycling within a cradle-to-cradle framework. This research introduces an eco-friendly and straightforward upcycling process for plastic waste, which produces significant quantities of reduced graphene oxide through a carefully designed 2-stage pyrolysis method. To enhance the electrochemical properties of the reduced graphene oxide, they were doped with heteroatoms (i.e. nitrogen and phosphorus) via a hydrothermal route. Also, as the nature of the electrolyte plays a significant role in electrochemical analysis, a comparative evaluation of the supercapacitive performance of the heteroatom-doped reduced graphene oxide was conducted across various aqueous electrolytes, including 1 M H2SO4, 6 M KOH, and 2 M KCl, as well as hydrogel polymer electrolytes such as 1 M H2SO4/1 M PVA, 2 M KCl/1 M PVA, and 6 M KOH/1 M PVA. Our results demonstrate that synthesized material from waste plastic exhibits excellent performance, particularly when combined with a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, achieving the highest specific capacitance of 407.6 F/g. In conclusion, this study presents a cost-effective and sustainable approach to promoting a circular economy by repurposing waste plastic for energy storage applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The effective functioning of modern smart devices critically depends on efficient energy storage solutions to ensure sustained performance over extended periods. In this context, supercapacitors (SCs) exhibit superior capacitance properties and an extended charging/discharging life compared to conventional counterparts, and emerge as promising energy storage devices with the potential to replace conventional batteries and other established energy storage systems [1–4]. Carbon-based nanomaterial (especially graphene-based materials) have emerged as a top choice for supercapacitors due to its exceptional conductivity, high power density, and excellent mechanical properties [5]. Among various electrode materials, graphene has been extensively studied and optimized for use in SCs. Enhancing the capacitive performance of graphene through methods such as heteroatom doping, functionalization, and composite formation has been a major research focus [6]. Notably, single-layer graphene, when fully utilized, can achieve a high specific capacitance of 550 F/g [7].

The addition of foreign atoms to the carbon lattice improves the wettability of the electrodes and produces electrochemically active sites that contribute to electrode pseudocapacitance, thus increasing the capacitance [6–9]. The carbon nanotubes were first treated with H2SO4/HNO3 to introduce carboxylic groups onto their surface. With a specific capacitance of 302 F/g at 1 A/g, the functionalized carbon nanotube and graphene composite material synthesized for the supercapacitor electrode showed encouraging potential applications as an electrode material for energy storage devices [10]. Cui et al. utilized activated carbon and graphene as raw materials to fabricate a sponge-like composite hydrogel for high-performance supercapacitors through a one-step hydrothermal process. The hydrogel showed a specific capacitance of 183.4 F/g, which increased to a remarkable 505.6 F/g after being doped with nitrogen [11]. Karthika prepared P-doped graphene by the activation of graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide with phosphoric acid. The prepared P-doped graphene exhibited a high specific capacitance value of 367 F/g, which was much higher than that of the P-free graphene [12]. However, this work did not provide information on the level and type of the P-doping within the graphene matrix. For instance, N-doping enhances the capacitive performance of graphene by modulating its microstructure and electrical conductivity while inducing surface functionalities that participate in faradaic reversible redox reactions. Recently, N/P co-doped graphene with 0.57–1.57 at % of phosphorous reached an enhanced specific capacitance of 244 F/g in an aqueous electrolyte of 6M KOH, compared with N-doped graphene [13]. It is important to note that the graphene-based materials discussed in these studies are typically synthesized using the modified Hummers’ method, which involves the use of chemicals such as potassium permanganate, sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, and graphite. These chemicals are costly and require extra caution [14,15]. Therefore, the concept of synthesis of graphene-based materials from low-cost precursors (waste materials) is gaining attention within the scientific community. In this regard, various reports are being published on the synthesis of graphene-based materials from solid waste (like agricultural waste, waste paper, and waste plastics) for energy applications [16–19]. Out of these, waste plastic–derived graphene-based materials are very fascinating, as they deal with 2 popular and rapidly-growing research topics – waste plastic management and the demand for renewable energy sources [20].

Pandey et al. pioneered the synthesis of graphene sheets from waste plastics using a 2-step pyrolysis method, where bentonite nanoclay was employed as an effective degradation catalyst [21]. Building on this work, Karakoti et al. reported the bulk synthesis of GNs derived from waste plastic and examined their supercapacitive behavior using various current collectors, including copper tape (CuT), indium tin oxide glass (ITO), graphite sheet (GS), and aluminum sheet (AlS), in a PVA-H3PO4 gel electrolyte. Their findings indicate that the AlS current collector exhibited superior performance, achieving the highest specific capacitance of 38.78 F/g with the plastic waste GNs [22].

Karakoti et al. further explored the potential of waste plastic-derived graphene nanosheets for supercapacitor applications. Their study focused on the impact of different degradation catalysts, comparing Montmorillonite nanoclay and ZnO. The findings revealed that MMT nanoclay outperforms ZnO, making it a superior catalyst for the synthesis of graphene nanosheets intended for energy storage applications. Supercapacitor cells are fabricated from the graphene nanosheets synthesized using ZnO and MMT, and show good electrochemical performance with specific capacitances of 72.8 F/g and 377.4 F/g, respectively [23]. These advancements highlight the promising role of waste plastic-derived graphene in the development of sustainable energy technologies. The objective of this research is to engineer the morphology of waste plastic–derived reduced graphene oxide (WPrGO) and nitrogen and phosphorus doped WPrGO (WPrGO-NP) to enhance the capacitance of supercapacitors (SCs). Additionally, as the electrolyte plays a crucial role, and no electrochemical study of WPrGO in different electrolytes has been reported to date, this work investigates various electrolytes—1 M H2SO4, 6 M KOH, 2 M KCl, 1 M H2SO4/PVA, 2 M KCl/PVA, and 6 M KOH/PVA—to determine which offers the widest potential window. This comprehensive approach aims to optimize both the electrode material and the electrolyte composition for improved SC performance.

We have used waste Polyethylene terephthalate bottles as a precursor for the synthesis of WPrGO, with bentonite clay as the degradation catalysts. Other chemicals like acetone, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) polymer were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. The graphite sheets used as current collectors were purchased from Madras Asbestos Stores.

WPrGO was synthesized following a method we have previously reported [3,21–23]. Single-use waste plastic was mixed with the degradation catalyst, MMT nanoclay, and 2-stage pyrolysis (i.e., 400°C and 900°C in an N2 atmosphere). The yield of the synthesized WPrGO was 15–20%. Following this, doping with nitrogen and phosphorous was carried out by the easy hydrothermal approach in the presence of ethylene diamine and phosphoric acid, as shown in Fig. 1. The detailed process is given in the supporting information.

Initially, the WPrGO-NP sample was homogeneously mixed with 10 wt% PVDF binder dissolved in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) using a mortar and pestle. The resulting slurry was then cast onto a 1 ×1 cm graphite sheet and dried overnight at 80°C in an oven. The weight of the active material in the working electrode, approximately 1 mg, was determined using a microbalance. The prepared electrode was subsequently assessed in a 3-electrode configuration, with a platinum wire serving as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Various electrolytes, including 1 M H2SO4, 6 M KOH, 2 M KCl, 1 M H2SO4/1 M PVA, 2 M KCl/1 M PVA, and 6 M KOH/1 M PVA, were employed to investigate their impact on the working electrode.

The investigation into the structural and surface characteristics of the WPrGO-NP samples was conducted utilizing a combination of analytical techniques. These included: Raman spectroscopy, using a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope equipped with a 514 nm laser (Renishaw Corporate, UK); X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), performed using a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA); X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, employing instrumentation from Rigaku; and field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), utilizing a Nova NanoSEM 450 (FEI, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a TEM EI Titan G2 80–300 ST (USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to analyze the samples’ thermal behavior, using a PerkinElmer TGA 4000 under an N2 atmosphere at a flow rate of 10 mL/min from 25 to 830°C at a heating rate of 10°C /min.

To investigate the electrochemical properties of the supercapacitive electrodes, an Autolab Metrohm workstation was used, by which cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were evaluated. All the experiments were performed utilizing WPrGO-NP with a range of different electrolytes, including 1 M H2SO4, 1 M H2SO4 + 1 M PVA, 2 M KCl, 2 M KCl + 1 M PVA, 6 M KOH and 6 M KOH + 1 M PVA.

CV measurements were performed on WPrGO-NP electrodes in 3 different electrolytes: 1 M H2SO4, 2 M KCl, and 6 M KOH, with and without the addition of 1 M PVA. The scan rates were varied between 2 mV/s and 200 mV/s to assess the rate-dependent capacitive behavior. The experiments aimed to investigate the interactions between WPrGO-NP and the electrolytes, and how these interactions are influenced by the presence of PVA and the scan rate. The specific capacitance (Cs) of the cells were determined from the CV curves using the following equation:
C
s
=
A
2 mk ∆ V
Here, A is the absolute area under CV curve, m is the mass loaded on electrode in grams, k is the scan rate in V/s and ∆ V is the potential window in volts (V).

A GCD study was performed to study the energy storage capabilities of WPrGO-NP in different electrolytic environments and under various current densities (0.2–3.0 A/g). The study evaluated the specific capacitance of WPrGO-NP in 1 M H2SO4, 2 M KCl, and 6 M KOH, with and without the addition of 1 M PVA, providing insight into the material’s energy storage capabilities and the influence of PVA on its electrochemical performance. GCD measurements were conducted at varying current densities, from 0.2 to 3.0 A/g, across different electrolytes. In a 3-electrode system, the specific capacitance is determined through GCD using the equation
C
s
=
I × ∆ t
m × ∆ V
where Cs denotes the capacitance, I stands for current density, m stands for mass loaded on electrode, ∆ t is the discharge time in seconds, and ∆ V is the working potential window.

EIS measurements were conducted on WPrGO-NP electrodes immersed in 6 different electrolytes—1 M H2SO4, 2 M KCl, and 6 M KOH—with and without the addition of 1 M PVA, aiming to investigate the material’s internal resistance/impedance and compute capacitance across different electrolytes in the frequency range of 10 mHz to 106 Hz.

The synthesis of WPrGO was conducted using a 2-step thermal treatment process, as previously detailed by our research group. Waste plastic was chosen as the precursor for its abundance and high carbon content, making it ideal for efficiently forming a robust carbon skeleton during pyrolysis, crucial for producing high-quality WPrGO. Initially, waste plastic was obtained from a local flea market, chopped into small pieces (average size of 10 ×5 mm) using an electric cutter, and then thoroughly washed with a soap solution to remove impurities. Then the small pieces of waste plastics were dried inside the drier chamber connected with hot air gun. After drying, this cleaned plastic was mixed with a degradation catalyst i.e. MMT nanoclay inside the mixing chamber for 30 min. The mixture then underwent a 2-stage pyrolysis process. The first stage involved heating the mixture to 400°C at a rate of 5°C/min in the inert atmosphere of N2 with the flow rate of 10 mL/min, with a holding time of 30 min, to eliminate all petroleum products while retaining the carbon skeleton within the pyrolysis chamber. In the second stage, the carbon skeleton was subjected to pyrolysis at 900°C in a nitrogen atmosphere, with a heating rate of 10°C/min and a holding time of 60 min. This step was crucial for thermally reducing the material and enhancing its conductivity by removing unwanted oxygen functionalities. To ensure the purity of the synthesized WPrGO, they was repeatedly washed with diluted HNO3, HCl, and deionized water. Finally, the WPrGO was dried in an oven at 80°C until all water molecules were completely evaporated. The final product WPrGO overall yield of was approximately 15–20% after washing, alongside the production of other valuable by-products such as gaseous and liquid hydrocarbons. The final product, WPrGO, achieved an overall yield of approximately 15–20% after washing, with a specific surface area of 339 m²/g [24], alongside the production of other valuable by-products such as gaseous and liquid hydrocarbons. To achieve this, 2 g of waste plastic-derived reduced graphene oxide (WPrGO) were sonicated in 50 mL of distilled water for 10 min. Next, 1 mL of ethylene diamine, serving as the nitrogen source, was added, and the mixture was sonicated for an additional 30 min. Following this, 1 mL of phosphoric acid, the phosphorus source, was introduced and gently mixed. The resulting solution was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and the reactor was maintained at 180°C for 12 h to facilitate the doping process.

Raman spectroscopy is the most promising tool with which to identify graphene-based nanomaterials. In this regard, the Raman spectra of the prepared samples before and after N, P doping were taken and displayed in Fig. 2(a). Two distinct peaks appeared at about 1344 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band and G-band, respectively, in the cases of WPrGO and WPrGO. The D-band is related to the disordered carbon atoms or the defects in the structure, while the G-band is associated with the ordered graphitic structure, and originates from the stretching vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms. Moreover, the intensity ratio value (ID/IG) indicates the defect degree of the materials. The ID/IG peak intensity ratios of WPrGO and WPrGO-NP are 0.90 and 0.98, respectively, which means that WPrGO-NP has more defects compared with WPrGO due to the incorporated phosphorus and nitrogen atoms in the graphitic plane [25].

In addition, the G-band was blue-shifted by 4–6 cm−1 in the case of the WPrGO samples compared to that in WPrGO-NP, which was attributed to the surface charge transfer doping, similar to previous reports [26,27]. To verify the reproducibility of the experiment, WPrGO synthesis was repeated 3 times, and the Raman spectra for batch 2 and 3 are given in the Supporting Fig. S1 .

The XRD technique was employed to investigate the structural characteristics and crystal phase composition of the samples. Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD patterns of both WPrGO and WPrGO-NP. Notably, there is no peak at 2θ = 10.11°, which is characteristic of the (002) plane of graphene oxide (GO) and corresponds to the formation of oxygen functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy [28]. For both WPrGO and WPrGO-NP, this diffraction peak at 10.11° has completely disappeared. Instead, a broad peak appears, centered at 2θ = 23.7° for WPrGO and 25.5° for WPrGO-NP, indicating the successful reduction of the material. The interlayer spacing of the material was determined utilizing Bragg’s equation, yielding values of 3.49 Å and 3.75 Å for WPrGO and WPrGO-NP, respectively. The shifting of the 2θ peak towards the higher 2 theta values and increase in d-spacing indicate alterations in the crystal lattice consistent with Raman spectroscopy data and the effective nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) doping in the WPrGO-NP sample. The broad nature of the peak suggests the material has an amorphous character, a feature that aligns with Raman spectroscopy findings indicating an increase in defects after doping [26]. Following doping with N and P, an increase is observed in interlayer spacing, attributable to the presence of N and P species, which alters the interlayer interactions within the graphene layers [13,29]. To verify the reproducibility of the experiment, WPrGO synthesis was repeated 3 times, and the XRD for batch 2 and 3 are provided in the Supporting Fig. S2.

TGA offers a straightforward and efficient method to simulate the thermal oxidation resistance of WPrGO under high temperature oxidation (HTO) conditions, allowing for the precise measurement of mass loss. Fig. 3 presents the TGA curves for WPrGO and WPrGO-NP. The TGA curve of WPrGO demonstrates the evaporation process of absorbed water with approximately 4 wt% mass loss before 200°C, followed by the pyrolysis of oxygen-containing groups, resulting in about 7 wt% mass loss between 200°C and 500°C. Subsequently, the graphene skeleton undergoes oxidation and decomposition between 500°C and 830°C, leaving a residue of 40 wt%. In contrast, the TGA curves for WPrGO-NP exhibit a single degradation stage corresponding to the thermal collapse of WPrGO, which is markedly enhanced by phosphorus and nitrogen doping. Notably, WPrGO-NP demonstrates a higher degradation temperature and retains more than 78% of its mass at 830°C, indicating superior thermal stability [30].

The lower thermal stability of WPrGO is attributed to residual defects and weaker CO bonds. In contrast, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doping in WPrGO introduces stronger CN and CP bonds, enhances aromaticity and graphitic character, and modifies defect sites, thereby significantly enhancing its thermal stability [31,32].

The composition and bonding analysis of WPrGO and WPrGO-NP were determined using XPS ( Fig. 4). WPrGO was found to contain 87.25% carbon and 12.75% oxygen. In WPrGO, the core level peaks observed at 284 eV and 531 eV corresponded to C1s and O1s [33–35]. In WPrGO-NP, the core level peaks observed at 284 eV and 531 eV corresponded to C1s and O1s, and an additional 2 peaks at 132 eV and 399 eV confirmed the presence of phosphorus (2.18%) and nitrogen (4.58%) in WPrGO-NP [28,36]. Deconvolution of the C1s peak revealed a dominant peak at 284.8 eV, attributed to CC/CC bonds of sp2 hybridized carbon in WPrGO, whereas in WPrGO-NP, this peak also showed similar characteristics. Another peak at 285.4 eV showed the presence of COC bonding in WPrGO, whereas the peak at 285.7 eV indicated CN/CP/COC bonding in WPrGO, demonstrating nitrogen and phosphorus incorporation into the carbon structure [37]. The additional peak at 288.5 eV in WPrGO and the peak at 288.7 eV in WPrGO-NP could correspond to CO or CO bonds (Fig. 4c and d). Similarly, the deconvoluted O1s peak in both materials delineated their elemental bonding configurations; their O1s spectra is shown in Supporting Fig. S1. The N1s peak exhibited 2 bonding configurations of nitrogen in WPrGO-NP corresponding to pyrrolic N (400.1 eV) and graphitic N (401.8 eV). The resolved P2p peak at 132.4 eV indicated PC bonding in WPrGO-NP (Fig. S3). These findings confirm the successful doping of nitrogen and phosphorus into the carbon framework of WPrGO-NP.

The morphologies of the obtained materials were characterized using high resolution SEM and TEM, as depicted in Fig. 5(a–d). High-resolution SEM images of WPrGO and WPrGO-NP reveal a sheet-like morphology in both samples. The TEM images of WPrGO synthesized in batches 2 and 3 are provided in Supporting Fig. S4. However, in WPrGO-NP, the sheets appear exfoliated. This exfoliation is due to the incorporation of nitrogen and phosphorus atoms into the WPrGO layers during the doping process. A similar surface morphology was also observed in the TEM analysis, further confirming the sheet-like structure of both materials, as shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). These structural modifications are particularly advantageous for supercapacitor applications, enhances ion transport and significantly improves the electrochemical properties of the N- and P-doped WPrGO, making it a promising material for energy storage applications [38,39].

To assess the electrode material’s suitability for supercapacitor applications, various electrochemical tests were conducted, including linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), CV, galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and EIS. Firstly, the LSV test was conducted to estimate the electrochemical potential window (EPW) of the material using a 3-electrode system in different electrolytes as shown in Fig. S5. CV measurements were performed at scans of 2 mV/s, 5 mV/s, 10 mV/s, 50 mV/s, 100 mV/s, and 200 mV/s for WPrGO-NP electrodes in different electrolytes ( Fig. 6). The CV curves at various scan rates as a function of current (A) and voltage (V) were studied.

Essentially, the WPrGO-NP material showed Electric double layer capacitor-type behavior in all but one of the electrodes, i.e. in 1 M H2SO4 + 1 M PVA, 2 M KCl, 2 M KCl + 1 M PVA, 6 M KOH, and 6 M KOH + 1 M PVA; the exception was 1 M H2SO4, in which it showed hybrid behavior, i.e. both faradaic and non-faradaic processes. The area under the curve is highest for WPrGO-NP in 1 M H2SO4, suggesting superior performance in this specific system compared to the others.

In Table 1, the specific capacitance values for both materials in different electrolytes at various scan rates are presented. The highest specific capacitance value was found to be 283.08 F/g at a 2 mV/s scan rate in 1 M H2SO4, suggesting it to be the best electrolyte for our studies (other scan rates’ values are highlighted in bold in Table 1 for 1 M H2SO4).

The CV results revealed the distinct capacitive behaviors of WPrGO-NP across the tested electrolytes and scan rates. In 1 M H2SO4, the material exhibited considerable capacitance, which decreased upon the addition of PVA, indicating that the polymer creates a potential barrier to ion transport. A similar trend was observed in both 2 M KCl and 6 M KOH electrolytes. Notably, the capacitive values in 1 M H2SO4 were higher than those in 2 M KCl and 6 M KOH, suggesting a more favorable ion-electrode interaction in the 1 M H2SO4 environment. The high ionic conductivity of H₂SO₄ enhances ion transport efficiency, thereby improving capacitance. In contrast, KOH and KCl have lower proton availability, which impairs ion transport and reduces overall capacitance [40]. The addition of PVA consistently resulted in lower capacitance across all electrolytes and scan rates, highlighting its significant impact on the electrochemical interface and suggesting a hindrance in ion diffusion to the active sites on WPrGO-NP. Furthermore, the decrease in capacitance with increasing scan rate across all electrolytes underscores the diffusion-limited process, a characteristic behavior in porous electrode materials where higher scan rates limit the ions’ ability to fully penetrate the electrode structure.
The electrochemical investigation of WPrGO-NP via CV has unveiled its promising capacitive properties and the significant effect of electrolyte type and scan rate on its performance.

The study also underscores the detrimental impact of PVA on the material’s capacitive behavior, likely due to its interference with ion transport. These findings contribute valuable insights into the optimization of graphene-based materials for electrochemical applications, emphasizing the importance of electrolyte selection and the potential drawbacks of using polymer additives in such systems.

The specific capacitance of WPrGO-NP in 1 M H2SO4, 2 M KCl, and 6 M KOH, with and without the addition of 1 M PVA was evaluated in a GCD study, providing insight into the material’s energy storage capabilities and the influence of PVA on its electrochemical performance ( Fig. 7). Table 2 displays the calculated capacitance values obtained from GCD curves for both the materials across different electrolytes at various current densities. The highest specific capacitance values are highlighted in bold. In 1 M H2SO4, WPrGO-NP exhibited the highest specific capacitance, which significantly decreased with the addition of PVA, suggesting that PVA impedes the efficient storage and release of energy, possibly by limiting ion access to active sites. A similar trend was observed in 2 M KCl and 6 M KOH electrolytes, where the specific capacitance of WPrGO-NP was reduced with the introduction of PVA. The decrease in specific capacitance was more pronounced at higher current densities across all electrolytes, reflecting the diffusion limitations inherent to faster charge-discharge cycles. This behavior underscores the importance of optimizing the electrode-electrolyte interface and the porosity of the electrode material to enhance ion transport at high current densities. Interestingly, the specific capacitance values in 6 M KOH were lower than those in 1 M H2SO4 and 2 M KCl, indicating that the ion dynamics and electrochemical interactions in KOH are less favorable for WPrGO-NP. The addition of PVA further exacerbated this effect, leading to markedly lower capacitance values at all current densities.

The GCD analysis of WPrGO-NP underlines the critical role of electrolyte choice and the detrimental effect of PVA on the material’s electrochemical performance. While WPrGO-NP shows promising energy storage capabilities, particularly in sulfuric acid, the presence of PVA significantly hampers its performance. These findings emphasize the need for careful consideration of additive materials and electrolyte composition in the design and optimization of graphene-based energy storage systems.

WPrGO-NP exhibited a specific capacitance (Cs) value of 407.6 F/g under moderate current density conditions (0.2 A/g). However, this value significantly decreased to 190.53 F/g under high current density conditions (3 A/g). The reduction in Cs values at elevated current densities is linked to the decelerated ion diffusion and migration across the electrodes and electrolytic interface. Conversely, elevated Cs values at lower current densities suggest accelerated ion diffusion and migration occurring at the electrode and electrolyte interface [41]. The elevated capacitance value observed in 1 M H2SO4 for WPrGO-NP can be attributed to enhanced contact at the double layer interface between the electrode and electrolytes. WPrGO-NP exhibits prolonged charge-discharge time intervals at lower current densities in comparison to WPrGO across all electrolytes. Furthermore, in 1 M H2SO4, WPrGO-NP demonstrates the highest capacitance value in both the CV and GCD plots, suggesting it to be the most optimal combination among the tested configurations. The observed trend of capacitances in both the materials across different electrolytes depicted in the GCD plot aligns with the trend observed in the CV curves.

EIS is a powerful technique for investigating the intrinsic electrochemical properties of electrode materials in supercapacitors ( Fig. 8). This study presents a comprehensive EIS analysis of WPrGO-NP in different electrolytic environments, aiming to elucidate the effects of electrolyte composition on the material’s charge transfer resistance, double-layer capacitance, and ion diffusion dynamics. The integration of WPrGO-NP in supercapacitors demands a detailed understanding of its interaction with various electrolytes, as these interactions significantly influence the device’s overall performance. EIS offers insight into these interactions by characterizing the frequency response of the system, providing information on both the capacitive and resistive components of the electrochemical processes [42].

EIS measurements were conducted on WPrGO-NP electrodes immersed in 3 different electrolytes: 1 M H2SO4, 2 M KCl, and 6 M KOH, with and without the addition of 1 M PVA. The frequency range was set from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz, with an amplitude of 10 mV around the open circuit potential. Nyquist plots were generated to analyze the impedance characteristics, and a circuit fitting analysis was conducted, as illustrated in Fig. S6.

The Nyquist plots revealed distinct semicircles in the high-frequency region, indicative of charge transfer processes, and a linear part in the low-frequency region, corresponding to ion diffusion into the porous structure of the WPrGO-NP. In 1 M H2SO4, a minimal semicircle diameter suggests low charge transfer resistance, attributed to the high ionic conductivity of H2SO4, enhancing the WPrGO-NP’s electrochemical reactivity. The addition of PVA increased the semicircle diameter, indicating higher resistance, likely due to the polymer’s insulating properties impeding electron flow. The WPrGO-NP exhibited larger semicircles in 2 M KCl compared to H2SO4, suggesting a moderate increase in charge transfer resistance. This is possibly due to the less aggressive nature of KCl, resulting in slower redox kinetics. Incorporation of PVA further increased the resistance, supporting the notion that PVA affects the electrode/electrolyte interface. In 6 M KOH, the semicircles were significantly larger, indicating the highest charge transfer resistance among the tested electrolytes. This could be due to the specific interaction between KOH ions and the WPrGO-NP surface, potentially forming a less conductive interface. The addition of PVA in KOH showed an even greater increase in resistance, underscoring the impact of PVA on ion transport and charge transfer processes.

The EIS analysis of WPrGO-NP demonstrates the profound impact of electrolyte composition and the presence of PVA on the electrochemical properties of the material. The findings underscore the importance of electrolyte selection in optimizing the performance of WPrGO-NP-based supercapacitors, highlighting the need for further investigation into the interfacial phenomena governing these effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic stability and percentage capacitance retention
ce:section.ce:para: A significant advantage of supercapacitors is their excellent long-term cyclic stability. To demonstrate this, Fig. 9(a) and (b) provide a compelling illustration of the cyclic stability of WPrGO-NP. These figures showcase the specific capacitance and percentage capacitance retention of WPrGO-NP within a voltage range of 0–0.9 V at a high current density of 10 A/g for 5000 cycles in a 1 M H₂SO₄ electrolyte.

Remarkably, as depicted in Fig. 9(a), WPrGO-NP exhibits only a minor reduction in specific capacitance, from 32.2 to 27.6 F/g at 10 A/g current density after 5000 cycles. This minimal loss underscores the robustness of WPrGO-NP under rigorous conditions. Moreover, Fig. 9(b) highlights that the WPrGO-NP maintains an impressive 97.2% of its capacitance after 2000 cycles and 85.7% after 5000 cycles. This high retention rate not only emphasizes the device's durability but also its potential for long-term applications.

This study introduces a novel and eco-friendly method for upcycling plastic waste into WPrGO, addressing the critical issue of plastic waste management while advancing energy storage technologies. The 2-stage pyrolysis process, followed by nitrogen and phosphorus doping via a hydrothermal route, yielded high-performance reduced graphene oxide from waste plastics. Our comprehensive electrochemical evaluation, using various aqueous and hydrogel polymer electrolytes (1 M H2SO4, 6 M KOH, 2 M KCl, 1 M H2SO4/1M-PVA, 2 M KCl/1 M PVA, and 6 M KOH/1 M PVA), demonstrated the superior performance of the WPrGO-NP, particularly in a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte.

The research highlights the significant influence of electrolyte choice on the supercapacitive behavior of the synthesized graphene. The use of 1 M H2SO4 provided the most favorable environment for ion-electrode interactions, resulting in the highest capacitance. However, the incorporation of PVA as a polymer electrolyte consistently reduced capacitance across all tested systems, likely due to the hindrance of ion transport to active sites. Previously, Karakoti et al. reported a specific capacitance of 38.78 F/g from a study of plastic waste derived graphene nanosheets in a PVA-H3PO4 gel electrolyte and AlS current collector. In comparison, the present work has achieved a significantly higher specific capacitance of 407.6 F/g, demonstrating the enhanced performance of our WPrGO-NP and optimized electrolyte selection.

This work underscores the potential of converting waste plastics into valuable graphene-based materials for energy storage applications, promoting a circular economy. By optimizing both electrode materials and electrolyte compositions (i.e., WPrGO-NP and 1 M H2SO4), the study paves the way for the development of efficient, sustainable supercapacitors. Future research should focus on further enhancing the material properties and exploring other waste-derived sources, contributing to the broader goal of sustainable resource utilization and environmental preservation.

Kundan Singh Rawat: Conceptualization, Experiments, Investigation, and Writing. Chetna Tewari: Analysis, Characterization, and Writing. Tanuja Arya: Editing and formal analysis. Young Nam Kim: Formal analysis. Prabhat Pant and Satish Sati: Electrochemical analysis. Sunil Dhali and Pushpa Bhakuni Negi: Editing and writing. Yong Chae Jung: Characterization and review. Nanda Gopal Sahoo: Conceptualization, Complete Review Editing, and Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Research Council of Science and Technology (NST) grant from the government of Korea (MSIT) (CRC23011-000), the KIST Institutional Program, and the Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi, India (DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/82(G)) provided funding for this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nxener.2024.100209.
